subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — President vs. Congress in Domestic Policy Making
stimulus: Over time, there has been conflict between the president and Congress over their roles in domestic policy making. Develop an argument as to whether the president or Congress should have more power over domestic policy making.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Federalist No. 51, Federalist No. 70, Article I of the United States Constitution.)
parts.scoring_notes: Thesis must take a clear position and establish a line of reasoning. Does NOT need foundational documents in the thesis.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: The president should have more power because presidents are more effective leaders who can respond quickly to domestic challenges without congressional gridlock.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: Congress should have more power over domestic policy making since members are the directly elected representatives of the people and reflect diverse constituency interests.

wrong_examples: The president should have more power over domestic policy making. (States a position without a line of reasoning — no 'because' clause or explanation)
Congress was stronger than the presidency in the nineteenth century. (Historical description — does not take a normative position on who should have more power)
common_traps: The thesis must state a POSITION (president OR Congress) AND a LINE OF REASONING (at least one reason why) — a position without reasoning does not earn Row A
The thesis should address DOMESTIC policy specifically — a thesis about foreign policy or war powers does not respond to the prompt
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row A (1 point): Responds to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. Must take a position on whether the president or Congress should have more power over domestic policy making and provide at least one reason. Examples that earn this point: 'The president should have more power because presidents are more effective leaders who can respond quickly to domestic challenges without congressional gridlock.' 'Congress should have more power over domestic policy making since members are the directly elected representatives of the people and reflect diverse constituency interests.' Examples that do NOT earn: 'The president should have more power over domestic policy making.' (No line of reasoning.) 'Congress was stronger in the nineteenth century.' (Historical description, not a normative argument.)

sample_responses.response: The president should have more power because presidents are more effective leaders who can respond quickly to domestic challenges without congressional gridlock.
sample_responses.commentary: Takes a clear position (president) and establishes a line of reasoning (speed and effectiveness in avoiding gridlock). Earns Row A.

sample_responses.response: Congress should have more power over domestic policy making since members are the directly elected representatives of the people and reflect diverse constituency interests.
sample_responses.commentary: Clear position (Congress) with a line of reasoning (direct electoral accountability and constituency representation). Earns Row A.

sample_responses.response: The president should have more power over domestic policy making.
sample_responses.commentary: States a position but provides no line of reasoning — no 'because' clause explaining why the president should have more power. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: TIERED SCORING: Award the HIGHEST tier met. Tier 2 and 3 require Row A. Tier 3 requires at least one piece from Federalist 51, Federalist 70, or Article I.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 1: Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: One piece of evidence relevant to the topic
alternatives.correct_example: Mentions Federalist 51, Federalist 70, Article I, executive orders, the veto power, the Affordable Care Act, appropriations power, or another relevant course concept about presidential or congressional domestic powers.

wrong_examples: Completely off-topic evidence unrelated to domestic policy making or the powers of the president/Congress
common_traps: Tier 1 does NOT require Row A and evidence only needs to be topically relevant — even a vague reference to 'the founders' intent' earns Tier 1 if clearly relevant
Students can earn Tier 1 even if their characterization of evidence is slightly inaccurate, as long as the evidence is topically relevant
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row B Tier 1 (1 point): Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. Does not require Row A. Acceptable evidence includes references to Federalist 51, Federalist 70, Article I, executive orders, the veto power, specific domestic legislation, appropriations power, or another relevant course concept about presidential or congressional domestic powers.

sample_responses.response: Hamilton argued in Federalist 70 that an energetic executive is essential to good government.
sample_responses.commentary: Provides one piece of topically relevant evidence (Federalist 70 and an energetic executive). Earns Tier 1 even without a thesis.

sample_responses.response: The legislative and executive branches both exist in the United States government.
sample_responses.commentary: States a basic fact about government structure but does not provide any specific evidence connected to the topic of domestic policy making power. 0 pts.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 2: Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence. Requires Row A.

required_elements: One specific piece that directly supports the thesis, OR two relevant pieces
alternatives.correct_example: Cites Federalist 70's argument for an energetic executive (Hamilton) to directly support the thesis that the president should have more power over domestic policy.

wrong_examples: Cites Federalist 70 without explaining how Hamilton's argument for an energetic executive connects to the thesis about domestic policy power
common_traps: Federalist 70 (Hamilton) argues for a strong, energetic executive — supports presidential power thesis
Federalist 51 (Madison) argues for checks and balances and separation of powers — can support either thesis depending on framing; Tier 2 REQUIRES Row A
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row B Tier 2 (2 points total): Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence. Requires Row A. Examples that earn Tier 2: Uses Federalist 70's argument for an energetic executive to directly support the presidential power thesis. Uses Article I's enumerated powers to support the congressional power thesis by showing the scope of Congress's domestic authority.

sample_responses.response: Thesis: The president should have more power. Evidence: In Federalist 70, Hamilton argued that a strong, energetic executive is essential to good government because it allows for decisive action — this directly supports the thesis that the president should have more power over domestic policy, since a president can act decisively without the slow deliberative process of Congress.
sample_responses.commentary: Has a thesis (Row A) and provides one specific piece of evidence (Federalist 70) directly supporting the presidential power thesis. Earns Tier 2.

sample_responses.response: Federalist 70 talks about executive power and Article I gives Congress its powers.
sample_responses.commentary: Names two pieces of evidence but neither is connected to the thesis — evidence must support the thesis to earn Tier 2. Earns at most Tier 1. 0 pts for Tier 2.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 3: Uses TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, at least one from a listed foundational document (Federalist 51, Federalist 70, or Article I). Requires Row A.

required_elements: Two specific pieces of evidence supporting the thesis
At least one from: Federalist 51, Federalist 70, or Article I
alternatives.correct_example: Uses Article I's enumerated powers (power of the purse, commerce clause, necessary and proper clause) to show that Congress has broad domestic policy authority, AND cites the gridlock that results from congressional supermajority requirements as evidence that Congress's slow process actually ensures more thorough representation — both supporting the thesis that Congress should have more domestic policy power.

wrong_examples: Two relevant pieces of evidence but neither from Federalist 51, Federalist 70, or Article I
Uses Federalist 70 to argue Congress should have more power (misapplies the document — Federalist 70 specifically argues for executive energy and strength, not congressional dominance)
common_traps: Federalist 70 argues FOR presidential power — using it to support a congressional power thesis requires careful framing that acknowledges the document's argument while explaining why it does not apply in the domestic context

Article I is a rich source for congressional power evidence: the spending/appropriations power, commerce clause, necessary and proper clause, and the veto override all demonstrate Congress's domestic policy authority
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row B Tier 3 (3 points total): Uses two pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, at least one from a listed foundational document (Federalist 51, Federalist 70, or Article I). Requires Row A. Examples: Uses Article I's enumerated powers (appropriations, commerce clause) and Federalist 51's separation of powers argument, both supporting the thesis that Congress should have more domestic policy power. Uses Federalist 70's energetic executive argument plus the informal power of executive orders, both supporting the presidential power thesis.

sample_responses.response: Thesis: Congress should have more power. Evidence 1: Article I of the Constitution grants Congress broad domestic policy powers — including the power of the purse (appropriations), the commerce clause, and the necessary and proper clause — demonstrating that the founders intended Congress to be the primary domestic policymaker. Evidence 2: When presidents have used executive orders to bypass Congress on domestic issues, Congress has pushed back through legislation and appropriations cuts, showing that even in practice, Congress holds the primary levers of domestic policy power.
sample_responses.commentary: Has a thesis (Row A), uses Article I (a listed foundational document) and executive order dynamics (course concept) as two specific pieces of evidence both supporting the congressional power thesis. Earns Tier 3.

sample_responses.response: Federalist 70 argues for an energetic executive, and the Civil War showed presidents need power during crises.
sample_responses.commentary: Federalist 70 is one listed document, but the second evidence (Civil War crisis) is about war/foreign policy, not domestic policy specifically. If the thesis is about domestic policy, the second piece must be connected to domestic (not war/crisis) powers. May earn Tier 2 at best. 0 pts for Tier 3 if the second piece is off-topic.

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A and at least 2 points in Row B. Must explain the relationship between evidence and the argument.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim using causal or analytical logic
alternatives.correct_example: Informal powers give the president the ability to make policies quickly through executive orders and the bully pulpit, so the president should have more power over domestic policy making because the speed of presidential action prevents gridlock from stalling critical domestic issues.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim using causal or analytical logic
alternatives.correct_example: Senate rules that require a supermajority for cloture ensure that the policymaking process produces broad consensus — this means Congress's slower process actually better reflects the diverse will of the American public than unilateral presidential action.

wrong_examples: The president can act quickly, so the president should have more power. (States a claim without connecting specific evidence to the thesis through reasoning)
Congress represents the people, so it should make domestic policy. (No reasoning connecting specific evidence to the conclusion)
common_traps: Reasoning must connect SPECIFIC EVIDENCE (already cited in Row B) to the THESIS — do not introduce new evidence in Row C
Requires Row A AND Row B ≥ 2 pts — students must earn both prerequisite rows before Row C can be awarded
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row C (1 point): Uses reasoning to explain how or why evidence supports the claim or thesis. Requires Row A and at least Row B Tier 2. Must use causal, comparative, or process reasoning — not just restate the evidence. Examples that earn: 'Informal powers give the president the ability to make policies quickly through executive orders and the bully pulpit, so the president should have more power over domestic policy making because the speed of presidential action prevents gridlock from stalling critical domestic issues.' 'Senate rules that require a supermajority for cloture ensure that the policymaking process produces broad consensus — this means Congress's slower process actually better reflects the diverse will of the American public than unilateral presidential action.'

sample_responses.response: Informal powers give the president the ability to make policies quickly through executive orders and the bully pulpit, so the president should have more power over domestic policy making because the speed of presidential action prevents gridlock from stalling critical domestic issues.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains the causal mechanism connecting the evidence (executive orders, bully pulpit) to the thesis (president should have more domestic power) through the mechanism of speed. Earns Row C.

sample_responses.response: Senate rules that require a supermajority for cloture ensure that the policymaking process produces broad consensus — this means Congress's slower process actually better reflects the diverse will of the American public than unilateral presidential action.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains how the structural feature of the Senate (supermajority for cloture) leads to the conclusion that Congress better represents the people's will in domestic policy. Earns Row C.

sample_responses.response: The president and Congress both have power, so the president should lead domestic policy.
sample_responses.commentary: Asserts a conclusion without using specific evidence or reasoning to connect that evidence to the thesis. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut or refute it — not merely acknowledge it.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts, refutes, or concedes it with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: Representation is important, and while members of Congress represent districts and states, only the president represents all Americans — this gives the president a broader mandate to lead domestic policy than any individual senator or representative.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts, refutes, or concedes it with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: While some people might argue that Congress is better for domestic policy making because it better represents the people, better representation could result in Congressional gridlock and slow the policy making process at times when domestic problems require swift action.

wrong_examples: Presidents should not have more power when it comes to domestic policy making. (States a position — does not describe an alternate perspective or engage with it)
Some people think Congress should have more power. (Only identifies the perspective without rebutting, refuting, or conceding it)
common_traps: Must both DESCRIBE the alternate perspective (what does the other side argue?) and RESPOND to it (why is it wrong, incomplete, or outweighed by your position?)
Requires Row A — without a thesis, Row D cannot be awarded
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row D (1 point): Responds to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal. Requires Row A. Must both describe the alternate perspective AND engage with it substantively. Examples that earn: 'Representation is important, and while members of Congress represent districts and states, only the president represents all Americans — this gives the president a broader mandate to lead domestic policy than any individual senator or representative.' 'While some people might argue that Congress is better for domestic policy making because it better represents the people, better representation could result in Congressional gridlock and slow the policy making process at times when domestic problems require swift action.' Examples that do NOT earn: 'Presidents should not have more power.' (No alternate perspective identified or engaged.)

sample_responses.response: Representation is important, and while members of Congress represent districts and states, only the president represents all Americans — this gives the president a broader mandate to lead domestic policy than any individual senator or representative.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the opposing argument (Congress better represents the people through district representation) and rebuts it by noting that only the president represents all Americans. Engages with the substance of the counterargument. Earns Row D.

sample_responses.response: While some people might argue that Congress is better for domestic policy making because it better represents the people, better representation could result in Congressional gridlock and slow the policy making process at times when domestic problems require swift action.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the opposing view (Congress better represents the people) and rebuts it substantively (better representation can cause gridlock when swift action is needed). Earns Row D.

sample_responses.response: While Congress may seem to have more power, the president also plays an important role in domestic policy.
sample_responses.commentary: Concede-plus-generic-reaffirm. Acknowledges Congress's power but responds with a vague assertion about the president's role rather than engaging with the specific opposing argument about representation or democratic accountability. 0 pts.
